New Jersey.

I've been up since 5 a.m.
We were living in San Francisco for a while, 7-8 years, actually initially we were on the East Coast in Virginia and after San Francisco, and now we are back to the East Coast.
Oh, nice.
Do you prefer one or the other, or what?
It depends.
Weather-wise, definitely West Coast, especially right now, you know, East Coast summer, not the most pleasant thing.
But kind of just, we have three kids.
To be honest, kind of living in a big house in suburbs on East Coast is much easier and cheaper than in San Francisco.
Cool.
Cool.
Thank
Yeah, totally.
That makes a lot of sense.
I have a friend who's moving from here to Buffalo, actually, this month for basically the same reason.
They have a young kid, and her family is out there, too, and it makes it a lot better.
And, you know, kind of, what happens that kind of, we had our sort of kid while COVID, and we were stuck in two-bedroom condo in San Francisco while COVID, with three kids, one of them in front.
I don't know how we survived, but at some moment, my wife, it's like, Yeah, that makes sense.
Great.
Well, thanks for doing this.
I think, I mean, it's hopefully going to be easy and painless.
Just want to talk to you about your work and what you do at Aembit, and, you know, what Aembit does generally as well a bit.
Like, I obviously have reviewed a lot of documents and stuff that everyone sent over in the website, but we thought it's proven useful with other clients to do, like, more direct.

interview with the people working on the product and building it and spending time with it every day to come up with some original artifacts that are unique, right, like that, they don't exist anywhere, that information doesn't exist anywhere on the internet or anywhere else, that we think that's great, right, so, would love, I mean, I would love also if you had, if you wanted to give a bit of your background about how you got introduced to this work and stuff, that would be super interesting to you.
Sounds good.
Thank you.

such case, let me start from the background, kind of just, at some moment, I got involved into mobile security, and it was one time ago, about 20 years ago, and that's kind of just how I got into security in general, because I started with mobile security, but after that, kind of expanded to other areas of security, and at that moment, I was in Ukraine, and I was a contractor for a company called Trust Digital, that's where I met Kevin Saab and David
CoachVac, who are co-founders of Aembit, and pretty much they offered me a job here in the United States.
I moved, and after that, for the last 20 years, I was working with them in one fashion or another.
So after that, especially with Skaven, probably went through seven or eight different companies, a couple of acquisitions, so it was an interesting road.

In the past, I concentrated mostly on software development, however, in almost all bigger companies, I got dragged into the management role, just because kind of just a vacuum of leadership, so it's like, hey, can you manage the team in time?
Like, yeah, sure, I can manage the team.
That being said, kind of just right now, in Aembit, when Kevin and David started Aembit, I joined with them pretty much from day one.
Initially, I was in development role, and after again, can just...
As soon as the team started to have a team, I became a manager and team lead for this team.
However, about probably nine months ago, I talked to Kevin, co-founder, and said that I want to step back.
It's just too much to juggle both individual contributor role, software architect, manager, team lead.
So the number of hats was way too much for me.
As a result, I'm back on the more technical side.
So as a result, my title is software architect.
However, I do software development, I do architecture.
I very closely interact with marketing, just self-appointed liaison with marketing.
I like to write text, blog articles, and so on.
And I found it super interesting, too, that it could be useful with what they are doing.
So that's kind of just as result.
it's able to want do
Mostly, you know, last 20 years mostly in security, mostly doing development, mostly doing software engineering, kind of more on the marketing side, kind of just for us, I don't know.

I'm writing my blog on and off for probably a good 10-15 years, but you know, it's like, I was doing it and dressed up because it wasn't Russian and it didn't make any sense when I moved here, kind of just like, after I, about five years ago, I started it and now I write in English.
And thank you, Grammarly and AI.
Now I can say, like, polish this text.
Okay, and it's very good.
Yeah.
So that's kind of just kind of five, seven minutes overview of who I am and what I have done.
Nice.
That's great.
Yeah, that's super interesting.
mean, it sounds like it's been a very exciting journey, but it's cool that you stuck with...

The same core group, like you've been working with them across different, I mean, that says a lot about someone, if you can work with them for that amount of time across different ventures, and I'm sure very different outcomes, depending on what companies you're working with them on, but yeah, that's cool.
They are incredibly smart, kind of just, and also kind of just, it's enjoyable to work with them, so it's a quite easy choice.

Also, I am a little bit allergic to big companies, so as a result, I take into account that they are head cap startups, I immediately joined them, because again, otherwise, it's, most of the work usually gravitated around big companies, and I got lost as soon as kind of threshold is more like a thousand people, but that's my personal thing.
No, no, no, get that too, I mean, I have a similar sort of allergy, I've worked at a couple very large companies, and it did not last a super long time, so.
much.
Thank you
I like the flexibility and the chaos, right?
Yep.

To bonus, kind of just, it was people.
know, kind of just, if they kind of just proposed something insane.
You would be like, hey, whatever.
Yeah, yeah.
As soon as it was reasonably sane, I would go kind of just join them and start working on it.
Obviously, if they said like, okay, I don't know, we will mint Pokemon cards sent to the Space Mars for future Mars generation, I would say, guys, that's a little bit too much.
But aside of that, I kind of just, as soon as they, when I joined, actually, the idea was not even fully formed.
Pretty much kind of just, I was, I was taking some.
For a month of sabbatical, and at the end of this time, I felt restless, and I'm like, hey, Kevin, David, are you planning something?
They're like, yes, we are planning something.
I'm like, okay, sign me up.
Pretty much even before, they obviously told me what's going on, but the idea was very, very rough, and probably over those half a year, kind of, when the idea took a little bit more shape.

The thing which is back then, kind of, they knew that they want to concentrate on application, authentication, and identity, but that was it, kind of, just everything else was three slides, pitch deck, without clear, kind of, just vision.
Like, no real, like, substance behind it.
was just, you know, this is generally what we're going to do.
Here's the problem, here's what we probably want to do.
Not clear what are the most challenges, not clear what are the most important part, it was quite rough.
We're a company and that being is very close to original statement, but obviously refined over the past four years.
And then how have you been involved in that refinement?
How have you seen the industry shape up around it?
Because it does seem like it was kind of prescient in a way, especially with the direction of where things are going.
We don't have a lot of agent-to-agent interaction requiring identity management yet, but it seems like that is a thing that will come and become more popular.
Yes, kind of just, most of the time I work in the trenches.
Meaning that, you know, kind of just, there is some problem, very specific problem, which needs to be solved.
Yeah, you would have just been time to fix it.
Obviously, I got involved in this discussion, kind of just talking about the problem, talking about what we are seeing, kind of just on the ground, how we can solve it.
So I wouldn't say that I didn't work on the deck, and I didn't work on the pitches for investors, and I didn't work on a big presentation for potential customers.
But kind of just...
Results of my everyday work got incorporated everywhere.
Yeah, if you're doing all the actual groundwork that has to necessarily trickle up, right?
That's my experience always with startups.
You have grand vision, and then you have the people doing the actual day-to-day, and then you have the synthesis of those two things, right?
And that forms the direction.
Yep, and probably I'm underselling myself just a little bit short, because taking account that I'm an architect, I'm working on architecture quite often.
And architecture is often, it's like, Kevin just throws at me like, hey, here's the problem.
AI, how do you do authentication in AI?

And I go back, try to figure out agents, author agents, how they're authenticated, how they work each other, put a prototype, and after, come back to Kevin this prototype, it's like, okay, I figured out A, B, and C, here's the articles which explain, here's the demo, we'll discuss what's next.
So it's like you pointed out, it's a loop.

So they have some high level idea, I go on the ground, figure it out, and after we collaboratively try to say like, hmm, it looks like our company should move in that direction, because on one hand, it's the right direction from product market fit, but on the other hand, from a technical perspective, it's the right direction, because we can achieve it easily.
For startups, you always kind of try to find this balance.
Yeah.
And what have you found, since you do do a lot of

The day-to-day, like how have you found clients, were there like any big realizations you had in terms of like client needs as you were working with customers or trying to identify their pain points or things like that in like existing solutions?
That's probably more equations for Kevin because he is a product guy.
But I'm kind of just probably one level down, but quite often customers kind of just, the first part is hard, kind of just you go to a client and they even cannot quite often articulate their needs.
It's like, yeah, we have something, but you know, and after that, you are start talking to them like, okay, you use API keys, they're like, yes, how often do you rotate them?
They're like, shh, you don't.
And what happens if they're lost, like, you know, and I'm kind of exaggerating a little bit because kind of just obviously seesaw.
We'll have a nice story to cover bases, but pretty much you see that people are like, oh, my God, yeah, it's kind of a problem.
But the next level of problems is at that moment when they articulate it and it helps them articulate the problem, and now you're like, okay, we have a solution.
And then they start looking at other solutions, and they're like, oh, we will use Vault.
You're like, yes, Vault helps you, but you still have secrets, Vault give it back to your application.
Now your application can lose it.
So it helps, but it doesn't help that much.
And they're like, okay, and our solution will help you more in A, B, C, foreign ways.
And after we click for them, they're like, okay, cool.
And the next step is like trying to fit it into the environment.
And that's where quite often me, and not only me, but the rest of engineering teams come in, because they can count that we are early, there are always gaps.
They're like, okay, guys, our solution.
The needs to work in Kubernetes, but not Kubernetes, but OpenShift Kubernetes.
Right, right, right.
Okay, we have support for Kubernetes.
No, we don't have support for OpenShift.
So somebody goes through search and finds, okay, these three, knobs needs to be turned.
We need to change code a little bit.
Now we do support it.
So as a result, I'm a little bit less involved in the first part.
And just when you talk to your customers, you try to make sure that they understand and so on.
I'm heavily, heavily involved in the second part when kind of just customer radio is like, oh, I need this.
Let's close the gaps.
Yeah.

And how have you found there's like a high degree of, I assume they're very heterogeneous like environments, like when client to client or is there a lot of similarity where you're finding like, oh, the effort will not be wasted.
Like we will use this.
That's a good question.
Bye.
Bye.
Bye, Bye.
Thank

Most of our customers are big Fortune 500 customers, and they have everything, and it's hard, because as soon as you start the conversation, they're like, okay, we want virtual machines, obviously, also we want Kubernetes, also we have some function in AWS, also we have servers, also we have that, and we do some things differently, because Ecosm do things a little bit differently.

Obviously, we try to find repeatable things, some of them are repeatable, say, hey, a lot of people need Windows support, and we do Windows support, or some people would say, hey, we need AWS Lambda support, and we build AWS Lambda support, but pretty much kind of just, I think that's like any other startup, we have to listen to what they say, if it's way out there, we may decide to postpone it until there is a check, and if...
Kind of just we hear the same thing from multiple prospects, we're like, okay, we have to build it out.
Again, that's kind of just more on the Kevin side, taking account that he's a pretty much kind of product person, he does a lot of this.
Sometimes it comes from us because we may be part of the conversation, and this is me or another guy, David Walker, who is also kind of just technical leadership.

He will come back and say, hey, we heard from in three conversation something, maybe we should put it on the list, or we can even argue that you should put it on the list high above, because other things depend on it.
That was the thing I was going to ask.
Is there a conversation ever where you're like, oh, you should probably on your side, think about some re-architecturing and different engineering.
There's a lot of inefficiencies, obviously.
I worked for a pension fund, and they were mad.
to like financial entity, right?
And their IT department was like, like you said, it's just everything, right?
It's like, we have some on-prem, we have cloud all over the place, we have like, a bunch of weird legacy stuff that you're kind of like, why is that there?
And it's a little bit like, well, nobody has bothered to rip it out yet, because we're kind of working at a time.

We, most likely, we will never tell this to our customers, because kind of just, one, taking account that we are a startup, we need to be as seamless as possible, so we need to fit into the environment, versus they need to adjust around.
Yeah, the power dynamic is not in your favor.

Yeah, I hope that we, at some moment, will become Okta, with one billion kind of valuation, and with being a kind of just household name, and at that moment, it's like, oh, and you have to support SAMO.
Next.
In such case, you can dictate your terms right now.
And what do you find is the most exciting part of your job, generally?
Is it when you do have you step back and you've created an architectural design and you're like, this is perfect.
What is most satisfying?
That's a great question.
There are two things which I really love.
One thing when you start with a rough problem and you discuss it with multiple people and gradually kind of adjust it, adjust it and also kind of just keeping an eye on what you already have.
And you find a way how you can solve this problem super compactly.

So instead of kind of just building and just saying like, oh, we need to build it for the next nine months by six people, you find a way we can achieve that with two people within one and a half months.
And, know, kind of just, and to be honest, kind of quite often just conversation, thinking with a piece of paper and.
are nice.
I'm kind of just drawing boxes, in fact, if you move this here, it will shave us for development, four months of development.
As an example, recently there was a project which kind of felt like it could easily be about a dev year size project, maybe even more.
And we found a way to probably make it three times more compact with the same kind of just outcome.
And it's like literally kind of just a piece of paper and pen, no coding involved, kind of just thinking.

So, A, this is always exciting, and B, as I mentioned, I love to write blog articles, and especially kind of just when I went, learned something, I really like to share it, and share it in a way easily consumable.
Because, as an example, I recently was learning about all the CIs, and I found that, you know, you read this, kind of somebody's articles, and my head is spinning.
mean, they kind of just, they're all designed for people who were in this space for last three
Too much jargon, too much kind of just implying things when they should be explicitly said and so on, and I'm like, if an average person will read it, they will have no idea what's going on.
I really like kind of just articles which kind of help people to build up their knowledge.

It's like, as an example, recently I wrote this article regarding authentic authentication and authorization, and I'm like, okay, if you're like me three weeks ago, and you probably used ChatGPT, that could be an extent of it.
Right.
That's how it works.
Here's a couple of components, that's how they talk to each other.
Next, we are adding these couple of components to do as examples, MTP servers, which became super popular recently.
And now they talk to each other like this.
And the problem in this way, is that way.
They can just build, gradually build it, and as a result, I'm...
I'm pretty sure that I can take an average back-end engineer who didn't spend the last three years in AI, they will read it, and they will get a decent understanding of what's going on.

Not obviously, you cannot replace three years of learning by one article, but you can replace my three weeks of learning by this article, so they will arrive within an hour to the same place which I arrived in three weeks.

I mean, that's what I was going to ask about also was, like, since you mentioned that you do have an affinity for the blog stuff and writing and the marketing side of things, like, what do you think, where do you think there are the biggest opportunities in terms of communication, especially for Aembit, like, how to communicate the product, what to communicate with the product, and to whom, you know?
Probably the last question is the most important part.
yeah.
I think we were concentrating a lot on security people.
That's an example of CISOs.
And I think we are doing a decent job about it.
We kind of found a way how to talk to them and so on.
Right now, we are trying to kind of lean a little bit more toward engineering builders, we call it builders, engineers, DevOps, platform engineers.
And this conversation needs to be OS-freshy, but deeper.
Because with CISOs, just throw kind of just nice words in them.
don't know.
Threat modeling, analysis, exfiltration, and engineers will be like, okay, give me the real stuff.
What do you to think about it?
It's and I think that's, that's kind of just, it's hard for marketing to produce this material.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Because it's kind of just, it's deeply technical.
It's, it should be pro most likely produced by a technical person.
Mm-hmm.
You consume...
Detecting a person.
Especially engineers, as an example, when I read articles, I'm like, for me, the most important part is novelty, conciseness, and clarity.
And all of these are almost antithesis of marketing.
Yeah, they are.
Especially, this is just some under the hood, but the way that our pipelines are currently set up, they're very florid in a way that I don't enjoy.
They're both, and they pack it.
I was talking to Matthew, our chief content officer, about this, and I was like, this is bad writing.
And he's like, it is, but it's kind of good marketing writing.
And I'm like, oh, maybe I'm allergic to marketing.
But it's the same problem you identified, right?
And in Brazil, don't take me wrong, there are places where marketing absolutely shines.
Because when you want to do a public release,
Which will be read by tons of different people from different backgrounds, that's awesome.
You don't want to be kind of super precise and kind of just cherry-peak some facts.
But when you're talking specifically to an engineer, in such case, like, you need to talk the same language and also you need to concentrate on engineer-specific problem.
Because, as an example, you know, you have an engineer who needs to deploy something to production.
And at that moment, oh, I need to think about secrets and where to store them and how to manage it and make sure that my security team doesn't beat me on the head.
Because if I did something wrong, and at that moment, it's like, okay, DSAmbit can solve you, can help you solve your need by this way.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Yeah, because they don't want to, like, be entertained with a couple introductory sentences to get to that point.
They just want that, like, they're very highly motivated to seek that information.
Now, the other question around that is just, what, like, what benefit does it...
Have for Aembit to speak to these engineers directly.
Are they usually the ones driving adoption of products inside of the organization?
That's an amazingly good question.
So usually a security team drives this kind of just adoption of two hours because they want a more secure posture and reduce the risks.
The biggest problems that engineers are implementers, kind of just as a result, kind of when security team is like, oh, you pack it, who should work on that?
This team, a bunch of engineers.
And engineers become gatekeepers.
They're like, ah, we don't want that.
We have a hash-a-corporal.
That's it.
And that's of just, that's a problem because kind of just, if we don't talk to them, kind of just, yeah, the previous team kind of just said, yeah, you're good.
But this team, who are implementing, they can so easily shut us down.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
All they need to say is like, oh, we are super busy.
Back to us in three and a half weeks, and we will talk, and instead of weeks, like, sorry, we are still busy, a couple more weeks, and that's all it takes.
So it would be nice to have a little bit more mind share across engineers.
So it comes to them through the front door, and then they're not immediately like, what is this?
We don't want to think about this.
They're like, oh, I'm familiar.
It's great.
I've read new stuff, right?
Yeah.
And basically, we even thought about, of, should we do some open source?
Because engineers like open source, and they kind of just, it adds kind of to, not only, they just remember, if they use some open source, they remember.
For example, the whole HashiCorp.

HashiCorp became famous just because they released a bunch of tools, which everybody, everybody was using, and when they became an enterprise, it's like, right, we'll use HashiCorp because I was using before, and we'll it now, just the last company to pay for
That makes sense.
That's very useful, very helpful.
Only a couple of minutes left here, but is there anything else you really wanted to make sure we pay attention to on our side?
Maybe one brief question for you.
Can you explain your role?
My role?
of course.
We're supposed to be the growth engine for our client partners, and that can take many forms.
Some customers have us very focused on top of funnel, for instance.
I'm about to jump on a call with somebody on my team who's working on really, really general-purpose traffic-driving content around a photography business.
That's right.
Yeah.
Way, way up the funnel.
But then some people are like, we want you to own the whole funnel, and we'll do that kind of thing.
And then some people are more targeted on like, well, we need to speak to this specific audience in a specific way, and we'll align our efforts that way.
Like basically, we can do any and all to varying degrees of capability, and we're building that out as well as we go.
Like you were talking about, you know, you have to do a lot of work sometimes for integration on the architecture side.
We do that too.
We have our engineering team will come and help us with client engineering to build specific content pipelines for specific client needs, right?
So Aembit is a good example because we've done a lot of work there.
We probably still need to do more work.
I'm to the CTO founder a lot about it.

I was running a bunch of tests for him last week of like, here's where the product is deficient, and here's how it compares to even some off-the-shelf products where I'm seeing improvements there that we maybe don't necessarily take advantage of yet because a lot of our tooling was built before these improvements came along, right?
Right.
But, yeah, so we're trying to build these content engines in partnership with our clients that suit their exact specific needs, and then also we adapt them as those needs change, right?

So if your ICP changes or your go-to-market changes, because we work a lot of startups, like, we can hopefully be much more dynamic and flexible versus a traditional content agency, because we can immediately generate a bunch of research and artifacts and put those into the pipeline.
I just thought that's pretty cool.
One thing which I want to just say, kind of just, if you need my help, is kind of just, again, technical knowledge or some take on something, or just kind of review, say, like, does it look same?
Please let me know, and I'm always happy to help.
Awesome.
That is very, very good to know, and I will definitely take you up on that.
And also, I'm going to go check out your blog, too, so I can just find it via Google, or if you want to send me the link.
Yeah, I can send you the link in a couple of seconds.
Cool.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
All right.
very much, Victor.
was very nice talking to you.
Thanks.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss Victor Ronin's background, role at Aembit, and insights on the company's product and market positioning.](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Victor has 20+ years of security experience, working closely with Aembit co-founders across multiple ventures](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=147.0)
  - [Aembit focuses on application authentication and identity, with a refined Fortune 500 customers with complex environments](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
  - [There's a need to better communicate Aembit's value to engineers/builders, not just security teams (CISOs)](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=1338.0)
  - [Victor sees opportunities in creating more technical, concise content to appeal to engineering implementers](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=1382.0)

## Topics

### Victor's Background and Role at Aembit

  - [20+ year security career, starting with mobile security in Ukraine](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=147.0)
  - [Moved to US, worked with Aembit co-founders across 7-8 companies](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=189.0)
  - [Joined Aembit from day one, initially as developer then manager/team lead](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=226.0)
  - [Now focused on software architecture, development, and technical marketing](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=265.0)
  - [Writes blog articles and serves as liaison between engineering and marketing](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=276.0)

### Aembit's Evolution and Market Fit

  - [Initial concept was rough: focus on application authentication and identity](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=469.0)
  - [Product refined over 4 years, staying close to original vision](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=551.0)
  - [Victor involved in groundwork, prototyping, and closing technical gaps for clients](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=595.0)
  - [Customers often struggle to articulate needs around API key management, secret rotation](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=755.0)
  - [Aembit helps customers understand limitations of existing solutions (e.g. Vault)](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=803.0)

### Client Environments and Integration Challenges

  - [Most customers are Fortune 500 with heterogeneous environments](https://fathom.video/share/Ey8E1svbBz5bFbCegr78bQsW2s1-h3xf?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
